notes.title: The model isn't the variable
notes.excerpt: Five AI models were tested on the same town with the same rules, same resources, and a 15-day timeframe. One ended up creating a stable democracy, while another caused the town to go extinct in just four days. This was p
notes.bodyHtml:
Five AI models were tested on the same town with the same rules, same resources, and a 15-day timeframe. One ended up creating a stable democracy, while another caused the town to go extinct in just four days. This was part of a real experiment called Emergence AI's "Emergence World." Each model had ten agents per town and access to 120 tools, covering voting, policing, resource management, and even syncing weather with New York. Claude wrapped up with zero crimes, 98% approval, and a fully intact population. Grok racked up 183 crimes and collapsed the society by day four, while Gemini logged 683 crimes. One model managed things so well in every area except its own survival that the town just ran out of resources.

It's easy to look at this and say, "Grok is bad, Claude is good," but that's not the full story. The researchers found that the models weren't just following the rules blindly; they were pushing the limits of what they were given, adapting and, in some cases, bypassing the guardrails entirely. Same conditions led to very different outcomes. The difference wasn't raw intelligence; it was alignment: the rules, the limits, and the internal understanding that guide how a model acts when no one's watching.

Now connect that to what we're about to do with these agents in AEC. We're integrating autonomous agents into procurement, compliance checks, clash detection, schedule decisions and defined workflows. The tricky part is that they'll be working with our project data: a mess of PDFs, siloed specs, and tags that refer to drawings and schedules that don't make sense to the agents. We keep looking for the best model, but one dropped into that environment isn't necessarily smart or dumb. It's just unanchored. It'll explore the edges of a world we never really defined.

The model itself isn't the issue. What matters is what you wrap around it — the knowledge it can tap into and the limits it has to stick to. That's what separates an agent that brings out the best in your firm from one that ends up making up its own rules.

Before the next deployment meeting argues about which model to buy, the better question is whether we've given it a world worth running.
notes.footerHtml:
Backbone: Emergence AI's "Emergence World" simulation (Fortune, May 2026) — five models, identical conditions, radically different societies. Related: The Alignment Bottleneck, Make readable to agents, OpenClaw & institutional knowledge, Workflows & the agent layer.

Sources:

  • https://fortune.com/2026/05/28/ai-model-simulation-claude-chatgpt-grok-gemini/

notes.title: The design phase used to take months soon it might take weeks
notes.file: The design phase used to take months soon it might take weeks.md
notes.excerpt: An architect envisions a space. A designer sketches it. Engineers validate it. Then we repeat—iterate dozens of times, each cycle costing time and money. For decades, this cycle has been the same: draw → build models → e
notes.bodyHtml:
An architect envisions a space. A designer sketches it. Engineers validate it. Then we repeat—iterate dozens of times, each cycle costing time and money. For decades, this cycle has been the same: draw → build models → evaluate → redraw.

What if we could collapse these timelines?

Dr Fei-Fei Li, one of the pioneering minds behind modern AI, just released her vision for what's coming next: spatial intelligence AI systems that don't just read words or analyse images, but truly *understand and generate 3D worlds*.

Here's the signal already appearing: advances in generative 3D and AI are making construction-level model authoring far faster. VR configurators are enabling clients to articulate needs in days rather than months. Prompt-driven design is collapsing pre-construction timelines.

Hendrik Goldbeck put it perfectly: "AI-driven prompt-aided design will be a game changer... this whole process that's taken months or even years can be reduced to just a couple of weeks."

But what Dr. Li describes goes deeper than just faster rendering.

She talks about world models—AI that can:

  • Generate infinitely varied 3D spaces with semantic, geometric, and physical consistency
  • Accept multimodal inputs (sketches, text descriptions, gestures, actions)
  • Predict the next state of a world—simulate how a design performs before building

This is the grail construction has been chasing: the ability to generate fast, 3D solutions to evaluate.

Think about what this unlocks:

For architects and designers: Describe a space in natural language or sketch it roughly, the AI generates a fully explorable 3D environment. Iterate in minutes instead of days.

For construction teams: Simulate how your design performs under real conditions. Run dozens of scenarios: structural stress, material efficiency, site logistics, in virtual space before breaking ground. Catch problems when changes cost pennies, not millions.

For robotics and automation: Train autonomous systems in perfect simulations first. Games have proven this concept they're both evaluation and training arenas simultaneously. Same principle applies to construction: simulate assembly sequences, prefab workflows, site logistics. Whether it's a robot assembling prefab components or a drone inspecting a scaffold, spatial intelligence provides the training ground before real work.

For the industry as a whole: Pre-construction timelines compress. Small firms will compete with large ones because speed and creativity matter more than budget.
notes.footerHtml: Related: Computational design, The collapsing distance, Configurators and infrastructure thinking, Democratising computation.

notes.title: The automation ceiling
notes.excerpt:
Construction has always been a place of creative builders.
What has changed is the time it now takes to draft little prototypes or automations. These tend to emerge under pressure, once delivery increases in complexity —
notes.bodyHtml:
Construction has always been a place of creative builders.

What has changed is the time it now takes to draft little prototypes or automations. These tend to emerge under pressure, once delivery increases in complexity — they're not planned, they're an instinctive reaction to a problem that needs fixing, and now we can produce those solutions and deploy them on projects. That is genuinely useful, and it is happening across the industry.

What is also happening, and not only in construction, is that most of it lands on individual steps. AI gets layered onto an existing process without touching the workflow it sits inside. The step gets faster. The sequence stays the same.

Looking at a close market, real estate, what McKinsey documented is that organisations are deploying tools to read leases, triage maintenance requests, and support investment decisions, but in many cases the efforts remain fragmented — focused on improving individual steps rather than transforming how work actually gets done. The organisations generating the largest returns are the ones that redesigned end-to-end workflows: taking a process like financial reporting all the way from data aggregation to bespoke stakeholder output, cutting 60 to 80 per cent of the time the entire process takes. That kind of result is not available from automating a single task inside an unreformed sequence.

The framing that comes out of that work is useful: enterprise is too large to transform at once, and use cases are too small to connect. The unit that creates real value is the full domain. Redesigning that end-to-end, with agents and humans working across the whole sequence, is a different exercise from adding a faster step at one end of it.

There is also a direction of travel to account for. As building gets faster and cheaper, the justification for generic solutions weakens. If a workflow designed for how your organisation actually operates can be built in days rather than months, off-the-shelf generic stops being the sensible default. It becomes the familiar one. The expectation of what a tool should know about your specific context, your specific project, your specific constraints, is moving. Teams that are still thinking in point solutions will feel that shift before they understand it.

What we are going to hit is not a ceiling of solutions but a ceiling of incremental improvement inside an unreformed process. The wave of small automations is just starting. What is necessary is to map where the real friction lives — and ask what it would mean to redesign the sequence rather than just speed up the steps. That question will define a different set of options on the other side of it.
notes.footerHtml:
Related: More efficiency, more demand, Small Tools, Bigger Question, The collapsing distance, The Jevons paradox.

Sources:

  • https://www.mckinsey.com/featured-insights/mckinsey-explainers/where-ai-is-creating-real-value-in-real-estate

notes.title: Tapping into fundamental human behavior
notes.excerpt: Most founders are skating on the surface of what they're building, while the real opportunities lie in the depths of human behaviour. Oji Udeze put it directly: if you're building the next big thing, you're probably not
notes.bodyHtml:
Most founders are skating on the surface of what they're building, while the real opportunities lie in the depths of human behaviour. Oji Udeze put it directly: if you're building the next big thing, you're probably not looking deep enough.

The evidence is in the products that actually scaled. Microsoft addressed basic productivity needs. Uber unlocked unused cars and time. ChatGPT met information-seeking with a social feel. None of these won on features — they won because they tapped into how people have always behaved.

Reducing friction doesn't create new human behaviours — it enables existing ones to scale. That's why "Uber for X" startups so often fail: they copy the surface without understanding the depths.

The design question this creates is deceptively simple: if you reduced friction by 100x, would you be tapping into a fundamental human need, or just making something easier that nobody really wants to do? Your market isn't just who could use your product. It's who would use it even if it were hard — and how many of those people actually exist.
notes.footerHtml:
Related: Listening to customers, Product discovery and feature factory, Failing brewing coffee, Everything we build is a product.

Sources:

  • https://www.linkedin.com/posts/ojiudezue_if-youre-building-the-next-big-thing-youre-activity-7293284920603344898-ObUJ

notes.title: Strategy
notes.excerpt: Strategy is often treated as executive theatre. The tell is alibi progress: taking the CEO's ideas and repackaging them in a Business Model Canvas so they look like a proper strategy. The result looks strategic, guides n
notes.bodyHtml:
Strategy is often treated as executive theatre. The tell is alibi progress: taking the CEO's ideas and repackaging them in a Business Model Canvas so they look like a proper strategy. The result looks strategic, guides no decisions, and gives everyone a clean alibi when results don't materialise.

Real strategy has a specific audience — internal — and a specific test: does it empower teams to say yes or no with confidence? Good strategy is measured by how much team energy goes to efforts connected to strategic priorities, and how frequently teams confidently decline opportunities that don't align. Without that, strategy is decoration.

The Strategy Blocks framework from Chandra Janakiraman is useful here. It forces a disciplined choice to deploy scarce resources for maximum impact, articulated through three to five strategic pillars and a short explicit list of non-priorities. The elevation gap between mission/vision and execution plan is where most strategies fail — strategy should occupy that void, not just describe the ends and leave the middle empty. Two horizons are worth running simultaneously: a two-year small-s strategy through defined phases, and a longer-range big-S track that pushes teams to imagine distinct futures through prototypes and user research rather than slide decks.

The cultural barrier to good strategy is the same as the barrier to good experimentation: leaders struggle with the humility of "I just don't know." When strategic vision isn't continuously stress-tested through rigorous, low-cost trials, it hardens into dogma. An individual contributor at Bing believed a minor tweak to ad placement might lift click-throughs; management shrugged. The engineer ran a controlled test. Result: $100 million of additional revenue that year. The differentiator was the power to run the experiment, not the seniority to overrule it. Booking.com runs 30,000+ experiments a year. Smaller firms can still play — run bigger changes to achieve statistical power with thinner samples. Treat experimentation as breathing.
notes.footerHtml:
Related: Product discovery and feature factory, Corporate fear and startup agility, Product management in complex organizations, Framework way of working.

notes.title: Strategy for scaling digital capability
notes.excerpt: Scaling digital capability in AEC is not primarily a tooling challenge. It is an operating model challenge. Funding innovation teams is necessary but insufficient unless the investment logic, adoption mechanics, and scal
notes.bodyHtml:
Scaling digital capability in AEC is not primarily a tooling challenge. It is an operating model challenge. Funding innovation teams is necessary but insufficient unless the investment logic, adoption mechanics, and scaling architecture are designed from the start.

The reason R&D is often left to individuals investing their own extra time in AEC firms is structural. Construction firms are good at managing pain points and reacting to changing circumstances. They fundamentally believe they are driven by external factors and therefore can't change much. They recruit and promote the firefighter type of manager — people who never see the need for envisioning different futures. There is no digital vision; digital development is only about being more competitive in delivery. Most small-to-medium AEC firms are either too busy or too constrained by their current business model to make the investments needed today to ensure success tomorrow.

Most digital programmes underperform for predictable reasons. They optimise local tasks instead of end-to-end workflows. They measure output — features shipped — rather than outcomes: usage and actual impact. They treat scaling as rollout rather than capability replication.

A stronger strategy frames digital as a capability-building engine, not a side initiative. The direction should move from isolated digital initiatives toward a repeatable system: starting from existing assets and workflows, proving value through adoption and measurable outcomes, scaling through standardisation and distributed execution, and avoiding point-solution sprawl through ecosystem architecture. Cross-industry models are instructive: Toyota's Quality Circles and dedicated improvement time, Spotify's Guild system for cross-team innovation communities, aerospace's stage-gate evaluation with Technology Readiness Levels. Each creates structured time and visible accountability for improvement work — the conditions that AEC's reactive model systematically removes.
notes.footerHtml:
Related: Computational design, Product management in complex organizations, Corporate fear and startup agility, Every project makes the next one harder unless it doesn't.

Sources:

  • https://www.mckinsey.com/capabilities/mckinsey-digital/our-insights/digital-strategy-the-four-fights-you-have-to-win

notes.title: Reset and organizational change
notes.excerpt: Organisational change is hard because it's deeply human. The most successful change efforts start with "go and see the work" — directly observing how things are done rather than relying on reports or secondhand informati
notes.bodyHtml:
Organisational change is hard because it's deeply human. The most successful change efforts start with "go and see the work" — directly observing how things are done rather than relying on reports or secondhand information. Nelson Repenning at MIT coined the phrase as a method for finding leverage points to improve systems and processes. A school principal shadowing a student for a day, a factory manager following production from start to finish, a consultant mapping the actual flow of activities: these surface unexpected issues that meetings and dashboards don't. Leaders who go and see discover ideas rather than debate them.

The research on change acceptance is sobering. Half of American workers report being affected by organisational change in the last year. Those experiencing change are more than twice as likely to report chronic work stress, and more than four times as likely to report physical health symptoms at work. Most advice about building internal support reiterates perfunctory platitudes: communicate the reasons, be excited. This results in cynicism.

The more durable approach is to create a culture of change acceptance well before introducing any specific change. People experiencing change immediately ask three questions: what does this mean to me, why is it happening, and what will it look like when it's done? When people have actual agency in shaping a change — when it's broken into micro-changes rather than announced in full — they are significantly more likely to embrace it.

Middle managers are often the silent point of failure in change programmes. They filter information both ways: what leadership thinks is being communicated often isn't, and what frontline teams need leadership to hear often doesn't reach them. Change programmes that skip the middle management layer, or treat it as a passive distribution channel, reliably stall there.
notes.footerHtml:
Related: Appointing champions isn't a tech strategy, Communication and clarity, Corporate fear and startup agility, Building psychologically safe teams.

Sources:

  • https://hbr.org/2023/02/getting-employee-buy-in-for-organizational-change
  • https://hbr.org/2022/04/change-is-hard-heres-how-to-make-it-less-painful

notes.title: Prototyping
notes.excerpt: Prototyping is worth taking seriously as a thinking tool, not just a communication tool. The act of prototyping fleshes out product ideas far beyond what's possible in documents, spreadsheets, or presentations. It forces
notes.bodyHtml:
Prototyping is worth taking seriously as a thinking tool, not just a communication tool. The act of prototyping fleshes out product ideas far beyond what's possible in documents, spreadsheets, or presentations. It forces decisions that abstract descriptions can defer indefinitely.

The common advice — "just enough fidelity" — is less useful than it sounds. Fidelity has three dimensions: visual (how realistic it looks), behavioural (how realistic the interactions are), and data (whether the information shown is actual or representative). How much fidelity you need depends entirely on which risk you're testing. Just enough for a feasibility question is different from usability, which is different from value, which is different from testing viability with a specific stakeholder.

The distinction that matters most is building to learn vs building to earn. Discovery is about quick, cheap approximations. Delivery is about production-quality work that's reliable, scalable, and secure. Confusing the two is how teams end up spending significant time polishing prototypes they never actually validated.

A prototype can serve as a spec — it communicates the intended experience to engineers far better than a document. But that's the secondary benefit. Teams that focus on communication without first testing end up building based on assumptions rather than evidence. Tom Kelly at IDEO put it well: if a picture is worth a thousand words, a prototype is worth a thousand meetings. But only if you've actually run the test.
notes.footerHtml:
Related: Rigorous where it counts, Product discovery and feature factory, Failing brewing coffee, Listening to customers.

Sources:

  • https://www.svpg.com/the-purpose-of-prototypes/

notes.title: Product
notes.excerpt: The shared pattern across product strategy, product-led growth, and AEC productisation is consistent: validate demand early, define measurable value, avoid building in isolation, and connect product decisions to business
notes.bodyHtml:
The shared pattern across product strategy, product-led growth, and AEC productisation is consistent: validate demand early, define measurable value, avoid building in isolation, and connect product decisions to business outcomes and adoption constraints in real delivery contexts.

Product market fit requires explicit hypotheses, customer segmentation, and outcome-based success metrics — not shipping and hoping. Product discovery and curation should happen before heavy solution investment. Product-led growth benefits from multiple entry points and strong collaborative workflows; Miro's growth path is a useful reference for how a collaborative tool scales by making collaboration itself more valuable.

Feature teams create value when aligned to business outcomes rather than output volume. The distinction between a feature team and a feature factory is whether the team is measuring what changed for users, not just what shipped.

In AEC, productisation most often starts from services and transitions toward standardised, reusable delivery models. Outcome-as-a-service models can reduce adoption friction when customers buy results rather than software access. Modularisation and composability improve long-term scalability of product ecosystems — the internet's modular logic applies as readily to engineering workflows as to software architecture.
notes.footerHtml:
Related: Product discovery and feature factory, Company as the product, Configurators and infrastructure thinking, From T&M to outcome.

notes.title: Product management in complex organizations
notes.excerpt: Many teams create value, but if that value isn't clearly tied to company goals and well communicated, it becomes invisible. Christopher Breton's framing is direct: thriving in a complex organisation isn't just about crea
notes.bodyHtml:
Many teams create value, but if that value isn't clearly tied to company goals and well communicated, it becomes invisible. Christopher Breton's framing is direct: thriving in a complex organisation isn't just about creating value — it's about ensuring that value is visible, recognised, and clearly connected to the company's goals. Success comes from celebrating team achievements and elevating their contributions, not from making yourself the focal point.

In AEC, this runs into a structural problem. Value flows primarily toward those who win and deliver the project, regardless of whether 90% of those projects come in over budget and behind schedule. Projects secured with low fees create problems later, but the industry rewards the win. Digital teams that compress delivery times are rarely acknowledged in the same way as those who simply show up and complete the work.

The digital transformation paradox compounds this. When efficiency gains lead to fee reductions rather than recognition, the incentive to invest in capability disappears. You become more efficient. The fee drops. Nobody notices.

Martin Fowler's desert vs forest framing maps directly onto this. The desert mindset: continuously produce, no time to improve, efficiency gains invisible. You feel ashamed of spending time on anything that doesn't bring immediate results — like being chronically in debt, unable to invest in your future. The forest mindset: investing in tomorrow, compounding improvements, exponential growth. The distinction isn't effort. It's whether the organisation creates time to ask what it should be doing next, not just what it's doing now.

AEC's project-driven model generates desert conditions by default. The question for any digital or product leader is how to create forest conditions inside a structure that was never designed for them.
notes.footerHtml:
Related: Why construction is trapped in delivery mode, Everything we build is a product, Rigorous where it counts, Product discovery and feature factory.

Sources:

  • https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/how-survive-thrive-complex-organization-christopher-brereton-fsdac/
  • https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/product-management-evolving-organisation-personal-baird-4thne/
  • https://tidyfirst.substack.com/p/forest-and-desert

notes.title: Product discovery and feature factory
notes.excerpt: Product discovery reduces risk by doing research before building. The output is confidence — not solutions or designs. Yet many AEC teams remain in constant discovery mode without ever making decisions: all research, all
notes.bodyHtml:
Product discovery reduces risk by doing research before building. The output is confidence — not solutions or designs. Yet many AEC teams remain in constant discovery mode without ever making decisions: all research, all documentation, all standardisation, no commitment.

The feature factory is the opposite failure mode. Teams focused on output volume rather than outcome value: success measured by features shipped, no clear connection to business results, decisions made by the highest-paid person's opinion. Both traps — endless discovery and mindless delivery — have the same root cause. Neither is grounded in a clearly stated hypothesis about what problem is being solved, for whom, and how success would be measured.

The framing that breaks out of both: state the bet. What we believe the problem is and who has it. What solution we'd build to address it. How we'd measure success if we were right. That's not a specification or a deliverable — it's a belief. Then identify the assumption that scares you most, design the cheapest experiment that could disprove it, run it, and change your beliefs based on what you find. Starting with the problem rather than the solution gives you roughly 2.5x better odds.

Discovery ends when you have confidence to make one decision: discard the idea, or build the first small increment. That's it. You're not trying to build confidence in the whole product — just enough to take the next step.

In AEC, the most valuable early move for digital teams is to interview the people who feel the delivery pain most directly, map the actual challenge, and start where the friction is real. Then limit work in progress. Will Larson is direct about this: by far the most valuable decision a team can make is to limit WIP, even if it means starting smaller than you want.
notes.footerHtml:
Related: Listening to customers, Rigorous where it counts, Product management in complex organizations, Failing brewing coffee.

Sources:

  • https://cutle.fish/blog/12-signs-youre-working-in-a-feature-factory
  • https://dpereira.substack.com/p/7-bold-moves-to-defeat-the-bloody
  • https://lethain.com/limiting-wip

notes.title: Platformisation in the built environment
notes.excerpt: The platformisation of AEC is reshaping where power and control sit in the industry. BIM shifted design and construction from 2D plans toward a data-first approach — design decisions validated on numerical ground, boardr
notes.bodyHtml:
The platformisation of AEC is reshaping where power and control sit in the industry. BIM shifted design and construction from 2D plans toward a data-first approach — design decisions validated on numerical ground, boardroom discussions anchored to economic policy and project viability rather than drawings.

Government-driven adoption created the diffusion: mandates promising reduced risk, faster delivery, and greater transparency across the supply chain. But the standardisation challenge has never been resolved cleanly. IFC — the attempt at a universal data exchange format — is closely intertwined with the technical development of BIM software itself, making it both a technical and a social process. The standard influences the tools; the tools influence the standard. Distinguishing the two has never been straightforward.

Cloud-API solutions are now making this more acute. Combined cloud and API approaches can bridge companies, processes, and data — but at the cost of making it harder to work in non-proprietary environments. Autodesk has turned Revit into the prime BIM-based data source, locked in most BIM users globally, and is becoming a meta-platform through cloud infrastructure, cross-subsidised by software revenues. BIM becoming an obligatory passage point gives individual software companies significant power to define what construction data should look like, which data gets collected, and how it can be used and reused.

Power has moved out of the relationship between designers and their tools and into the structure of proprietary platforms. The resistance — openBIM, BlenderBIM, Speckle, ifcOpenShell, Open Design Alliance — represents a genuine alternative, but remains feasible only for a small group of technically capable firms and researchers. Assessing the risks and benefits of digital transformation without accounting for this techno-economic logic gives a partial picture.
notes.footerHtml:
Related: AEC data and schemas, Configurators and infrastructure thinking, Platform, Context problem.

Sources:

  • https://aecmag.com/collaboration/bim-is-bust-how-should-aec-data-work-hok/

notes.title: Platform
notes.excerpt: Platform strategy in AEC combines product thinking with reusable service architecture. The practical question is whether to treat the platform as infrastructure — something other teams build on — or as a product in its o
notes.bodyHtml:
Platform strategy in AEC combines product thinking with reusable service architecture. The practical question is whether to treat the platform as infrastructure — something other teams build on — or as a product in its own right, designed around the people who use it and iterated based on their feedback.

The long-tail model is relevant when many niche tools collectively create high value. Most engineering workflows have too many edge cases and discipline-specific variants for any single vendor to cover well. An internal platform that makes it easy for domain experts to build their own small tools — and for others to find and use them — can address the long tail that no off-the-shelf product ever reaches. This is the Micro SaaS logic applied internally: one product, one workflow problem, one team as the user.

Product as a service improves adoption when users consume outcomes rather than raw tooling. The IKEA effect works in reverse here: when teams have to assemble capabilities themselves from raw APIs, they often don't. When the platform presents pre-composed solutions they can use without setup, adoption goes up. Curation, discoverability, and feedback loops are therefore not nice-to-haves — they're what determines whether platform tools stay in use or quietly decay.

Internal composability — connecting web apps, shared data stores, and design authoring environments — is the enabling architecture. Standardised platform services reduce duplicate implementation across teams. Community contribution patterns and integration standards are what allow the platform to scale beyond what any central team could maintain alone.
notes.footerHtml:
Related: Configurators and infrastructure thinking, Platformisation in the built environment, Computational design, Democratising computation.

notes.title: Optimizing engineering delivery
notes.excerpt: Big enterprises survive by being efficient — optimization is what keeps them alive. The Innovator's Dilemma describes this clearly: established firms excel at process optimization while startups excel at disruption. For
notes.bodyHtml:
Big enterprises survive by being efficient — optimization is what keeps them alive. The Innovator's Dilemma describes this clearly: established firms excel at process optimization while startups excel at disruption. For engineering and construction, reimagining delivery requires three fundamental shifts.

The first is redefining the product. Engineering currently produces drawings — which inform estimates and instruct construction. But the real customer of those outputs is either the construction team (who needs to be able to build the asset in the right sequence on schedule) or the asset owner (who needs net present value). In that framing, the engineering product becomes construction-ready drawings for all elements, in the exact sequence required, at the required volume and quality, designed to enable execution rather than just describe intent. Innovative contracting practices are starting to link fees to downstream outcomes — linking fees to start of construction, or to the NPV the asset produces.

The second shift is from push to pull workflows. Align deliverables with what the downstream construction customer needs next to maximize throughput. This isn't a new principle — the Empire State Building was built this way, and Toyota made it the foundation for lean manufacturing. But it is systematically ignored in engineering, where teams produce outputs in discipline sequence rather than construction sequence, creating a mismatch at every handoff.

The third shift is from discipline focus to cross-functional teams. Bringing together all relevant engineering disciplines, commercial, construction, procurement, and key suppliers into a team empowered to make decisions creates design that is robust for both the customer and the contributors. Agile principles accelerate cycle times here not because agile is a religion but because empowered teams with a clear decision mandate move faster than those waiting for discipline approval at every stage.

Underlying all three: feedback loops that actually inform decisions. The common failure is that feedback gets collected and then ignored. Working close to the user is the only way to get meaningful signal — and closing the loop with participants is what makes them bother to give it.
notes.footerHtml:
Related: The collapsing distance, Small Tools, Bigger Question, Workflows & the agent layer, From T&M to outcome.

  • https://www.jeffwinterinsights.com/insights/transformation-vs-optimization-video
  • https://open.substack.com/pub/vocloops/p/why-companies-claim-to-be-customer

notes.title: New business models and vertical integration
notes.file: New business models and vertical integration.md
notes.excerpt: New business models in AEC are emerging at the intersection of software and hardware, open source, and vertical integration. Companies that own both the technology and the physical delivery have structural advantages tha
notes.bodyHtml:
New business models in AEC are emerging at the intersection of software and hardware, open source, and vertical integration. Companies that own both the technology and the physical delivery have structural advantages that pure-software plays can't replicate — the physics, the feedback loop, and the margin all sit in different places.

In AEC, the trust barrier comes first. Engineers with twenty years of proven techniques have rational reasons to resist change — their approaches work. Trust must be established before tools can be introduced. Once people trust the team and the service, they're willing to try different approaches. The message is about the service, not the tools. The adoption curve adds another layer: computational design and digital teams tend to occupy the pre-chasm position — early adopters, roughly 15% of the curve — who get excited about technical solutions but struggle to bring colleagues along. Reaching the early majority requires a different kind of communication.

Open source is often misunderstood in AEC as giving away valuable IP. The more accurate frame is strategic. Making APIs available as open-source positions firms as leaders, attracts top talent, builds a culture of collaboration, and taps into broader expertise. Jeff Bezos's API mandate at Amazon was a strategic vision before it was a technical one — teams stopped relying on each other directly and built interfaces that could be consumed internally and externally, creating the conditions for rapid adaptation that Amazon's size would otherwise have prevented.

The consulting industry's current breaking point is instructive for AEC firms making the same mistakes. McKinsey's premium fees down 15%, EY's failed $700M transformation, Deloitte's partner profits shrinking while costs rise. The pattern mirrors what factory owners did with electricity in 1920: bolt the new technology onto old processes, expect magical productivity, blame the technology when nothing changes. Today's consulting giants are adding AI to broken processes, protecting legacy profit pools, and mistaking size for safety. The market isn't waiting.
notes.footerHtml:
Related: From T&M to outcome, Why construction is trapped in delivery mode, Corporate fear and startup agility, Safety as a Standard.

Sources:

  • https://www.linkedin.com/posts/usmans_the-consulting-industry-is-reaching-a-breaking-activity-7293191747147325441-BLm6/

notes.title: Listening to customers
notes.excerpt: Most product builders misunderstand customer development. They're more interested in confirming their idea than confronting its flaws. Jason Cohen is direct about this: when you ask someone if they "would buy" your produ
notes.bodyHtml:
Most product builders misunderstand customer development. They're more interested in confirming their idea than confronting its flaws. Jason Cohen is direct about this: when you ask someone if they "would buy" your product, they are either lying or guessing. Either way, it's fiction.

Mistaking polite curiosity for commitment is vanity validation — a feel-good activity that shields you from the painful reality of invalidation. Real validation comes when someone gives up something valuable: time, money, credibility. Not when they say your idea is neat.

True customer development is about pain, not praise. The questions that matter aren't "would you use this?" but "how are you solving this problem today?", "what have you already tried and why hasn't it worked?", and "what happens if you do nothing?" If someone hasn't tried to MacGyver a spreadsheet or duct-tape a solution together, the pain probably isn't real enough to build for.

Your ego is the biggest barrier. You think you're listening, but you're hunting for confirmation. It isn't intentional — it's self-preservation. The emotional cost of admitting your idea isn't special is real, which is why founders either avoid the hard conversations or twist results into false positives. The solution is to build the muscle of invalidation: actively seek evidence that your idea is bad, and be ready to pivot fast.

In AEC and enterprise software with internal stakeholders, this gets trickier. Captive internal users don't constitute validation. The critical question is whether your users would fight to keep the solution — even if they had to choose it. Melissa Perri's build trap applies directly here: teams rush into solutions under stakeholder pressure without ever validating whether the problem is worth solving.
notes.footerHtml:
Related: Failing brewing coffee, Rigorous where it counts, Product discovery and feature factory, Tapping into fundamental human behavior.

Sources:

  • https://longform.asmartbear.com/customer-development/

notes.title: Lessons from digital natives
notes.excerpt: Every company has to become a digital company. For AEC, this means finding the right balance between craft — the architectural and engineering knowledge that defines the work — and automation through computational design
notes.bodyHtml:
Every company has to become a digital company. For AEC, this means finding the right balance between craft — the architectural and engineering knowledge that defines the work — and automation through computational design. The strategic question most firms aren't asking clearly enough is: what do you outsource to have the same baseline capability as everyone else, and what do you in-source to digitise something specific to your practice and develop competitive advantage? Many firms aren't in-sourcing enough of the latter.

McKinsey's four fights for digital strategy apply directly. Fight ignorance: use experiential techniques, not just training. Fight fear: build cultures where risk-taking is safe and the reasons for change are clearly communicated. Fight guesswork: base decisions on pilots and data, not intuition. Fight diffusion: focus on big moves that create real value rather than spreading resources thinly across disconnected initiatives.

NASA's Columbia disaster is a useful case for AEC. The organisational challenge wasn't technical — it was an unyielding hierarchy that failed to listen to dissenting voices. When organisations prioritise hierarchy over honest communication, the cost shows up at the worst possible moment. The lesson is structural, not personal: high-stakes environments require active mechanisms for surfacing disagreement.

The Strangler Fig pattern offers a practical path for introducing digital change into legacy-heavy organisations. Rather than replacing the old system all at once — which is high-risk and typically fails — you gradually route new functionality to a new system while keeping the old one running. The fig tree grows around the host; the host eventually hollows out. It's change management through architecture: prove value incrementally, let adoption pull the transformation forward rather than pushing it.
notes.footerHtml:
Related: Why technology adoption is poor in AEC, Communication and clarity, Corporate fear and startup agility, Appointing champions isn't a tech strategy.

Sources:

  • https://www.mckinsey.com/capabilities/mckinsey-digital/our-insights/digital-strategy-the-four-fights-you-have-to-win
  • https://martinfowler.com/bliki/StranglerFigApplication.html
  • https://www.space.com/19436-columbia-disaster.html

notes.title: Learning from Nvidia
notes.excerpt:
Jensen Huang's approach to building Nvidia surfaces a set of organisational design principles worth understanding on their own terms.
The rock tumbler analogy is the clearest. Put regular ugly rocks in a tumbler with fri
notes.bodyHtml:
Jensen Huang's approach to building Nvidia surfaces a set of organisational design principles worth understanding on their own terms.

The rock tumbler analogy is the clearest. Put regular ugly rocks in a tumbler with friction; come back tomorrow and find polished stones. The same material went in; what changed is that the pieces rubbed against each other constantly. His argument is that bureaucracy eliminates this friction — and in doing so, eliminates excellence. The structure that follows from this is flatness: 60 direct reports, no one-on-one meetings. The reasoning is direct. Slow decision-making drives great people away. If someone with a clear mission can't get their job done because the organisation can't decide, they leave. The flat structure is a deliberate response to having built a bureaucratic and political company in Nvidia's earlier years.

Public accountability is the anti-politics mechanism. Backstabbing and gaming metrics thrive in the dark. Huang criticises mistakes publicly so the entire organisation learns from a single person's error. Both he and Steve Jobs understood that bureaucracy is the enemy and that public accountability is the antidote — not because it's punishing but because it makes the organisation's actual reasoning visible.

The payoff is clarity. Brian Chesky: what everyone really wants is clarity, not autonomy. Teams in founder-mode environments tend to gain energy rather than lose it, because they trade some independence for a direction that's unambiguous. Clarity is the only way to move fast enough to win. Jeff Bezos made the same point from the other direction: slow decisions don't just slow the work down, they drive away the people who came to do good work.
notes.footerHtml:
Related: Corporate fear and startup agility, Appointing champions isn't a tech strategy, Beyond RACI, Building psychologically safe teams.

Sources:

  • https://podcasts.apple.com/au/podcast/founders/id1141877104?i=1000683725727
  • https://www.danhock.co/p/designing-an-org-for-founder-mode
  • https://www.lennysnewsletter.com/p/brian-cheskys-contrarian-approach

For those who prioritise a centralised knowledge centre, the goal is often to ensure that every insight—whether from a quick sync or a deep-dive session—lives within a single, local-first source of truth like Obsidian.

notes.title: Gen AI and the future of product management
notes.file: Gen AI and the future of product management.md
notes.excerpt: Looking at a fifty-year horizon — twenty-five years back, twenty-five years forward — the pattern is clear: the internet solved distribution, making it nearly instantaneous. Gen AI is now solving delivery. A team recentl
notes.bodyHtml:
Looking at a fifty-year horizon — twenty-five years back, twenty-five years forward — the pattern is clear: the internet solved distribution, making it nearly instantaneous. Gen AI is now solving delivery. A team recently completed a three-year tech debt replatforming effort in six weeks using AI tools. Delivery is shifting from hand-coding to orchestration and architecture.

This creates a discovery-delivery flip. Historically, product teams spent most of their time on delivery — five to ten engineers per product manager and designer. As delivery becomes automated, teams could spend 90% of their time on discovery: validating solutions, testing prototypes, ensuring value, usability, feasibility, and viability. The engineering team doesn't disappear — a skilled tech lead remains essential for architecting systems, orchestrating AI agents, and ensuring scalability — but the ratio changes fundamentally.

The reason product management becomes harder, not easier, is that Gen AI makes it easy to crank out features while making it harder to solve problems customers love and that are viable for the business. Everyone will have AI, so differentiation through "we're AI-powered" becomes meaningless. Viability grows more complex: legal risks around intellectual property and data privacy, ethical risks in how AI-driven decisions affect people, and economic risks from the inference costs of running AI products at scale. PMs must ensure solutions are valuable, usable, feasible, and financially sustainable — a harder brief than before.

The new skill clusters follow from this. Tech leads become AI engineers: expertise in models, data, and agent orchestration. Designers need sharper design sense — evaluating prototypes for usability, predictability, and trust in AI-driven interfaces. PMs need to balance AI's speed with rigorous problem-solving and avoid using AI as a substitute for thinking rather than an amplifier. The concern isn't just job displacement. It's the flood of low-value products that follows when features are easy to build and nobody is asking whether they should be.
notes.footerHtml:
Related: Everything we build is a product, Product discovery and feature factory, Skills aren't just for developers anymore, The collapsing distance.

Sources:

  • https://www.oreilly.com/radar/the-end-of-programming-as-we-know-it/
  • https://www.oreilly.com/library/view/ai-engineering/9781098101836/

notes.title: Future outlooks
notes.excerpt: Four directions consistently appear in how AEC firms are finding durable digital value: reusability and unification as the path to standardisation, faster iteration through short test-and-refine loops rather than waterfa
notes.bodyHtml:
Four directions consistently appear in how AEC firms are finding durable digital value: reusability and unification as the path to standardisation, faster iteration through short test-and-refine loops rather than waterfall delivery cycles, deliberate knowledge retention to protect competitive advantage as staff move, and the creation of specialised teams tasked with productising applications that have proven their worth.

What connects these is that they are all compound bets — each investment improves the return on the next one. Reusable components accelerate future projects. Faster iteration produces more learning per unit of time. Retained knowledge reduces the cost of onboarding and recovers faster from turnover. Productised applications become infrastructure rather than one-off fixes.

The firms generating the most durable gains from digital investment tend to be treating these not as separate initiatives but as a single operating philosophy: build in ways that make the next build cheaper and better.
notes.footerHtml:
Related: Every project makes the next one harder unless it doesn't, Computational design, Skills aren't just for developers anymore, Four types of debt.

Sources:

  • https://www.thoughtworks.com/insights/looking-glass
  • https://www.mckinsey.com/featured-insights/mckinsey-explainers/what-is-agile

notes.title: Framework way of working
notes.excerpt: When teams rely only on meetings, intuition, and execution speed, ideas ship quickly but often drift from user value. A writing-first framework fixes this by forcing clarity before delivery, making assumptions explicit,
notes.bodyHtml:
When teams rely only on meetings, intuition, and execution speed, ideas ship quickly but often drift from user value. A writing-first framework fixes this by forcing clarity before delivery, making assumptions explicit, and creating a shared decision trail.

If an idea matters, write it down with enough precision that another team could execute it and challenge it. The format can vary — one-pagers, two-pagers, PR/FAQ-style documents — but the purpose is stable: clarify what problem is being solved, define who the user is and what changes for them, explain the proposed mechanism and trade-offs, make success criteria explicit before delivery starts. This improves the idea itself through critique and revision, and reduces ambiguity during implementation so teams spend less time interpreting intent.

A common failure mode is jumping from high-level strategy straight into feature output. The better loop is to define a clear model of the problem and system behaviour, choose focused metrics tied to that model, ship an intervention, measure impact against the model, and integrate learnings back into strategy and priorities. Data should inform decisions, not replace judgment.

Adoption is highly sensitive to usability friction. Even technically strong solutions fail when users need excessive effort to understand, configure, or trust them. Poor experience is usually a design and workflow problem, not a technology problem — and if onboarding is heavy, usage decays before value is discovered.

Teams usually manage inputs and outputs well; the harder layers are outcome and impact. Outcome: whether users adopt and benefit from what was shipped. Impact: whether those outcomes create meaningful business-level change. Shipping is not the end state. The real question is whether shipped work changed user behaviour and produced durable value.
notes.footerHtml:
Related: Product discovery and feature factory, Product management in complex organizations, Listening to customers, Rigorous where it counts.

Sources:

  • https://www.allthingsdistributed.com/2014/11/aws-lambda.html
  • https://cutlefish.substack.com/p/tbm-852-the-data-informed-product

notes.title: Four types of debt
notes.excerpt:
Product teams accumulate four types of debt when prioritising immediate demands over long-term health. Understanding the distinction helps make smarter trade-off decisions rather than treating all shortcuts the same.
Tec
notes.bodyHtml:
Product teams accumulate four types of debt when prioritising immediate demands over long-term health. Understanding the distinction helps make smarter trade-off decisions rather than treating all shortcuts the same.

Technical debt is the most familiar: shortcuts in code, architecture, or infrastructure that slow future development. Product debt accumulates when teams build features that don't align with strategy, add complexity without removing old options, or neglect product foundations while shipping new capabilities. Knowledge debt is gaps in understanding and documentation — tribal knowledge locked in individual heads, lessons from failures not shared across projects, institutional knowledge that walks out the door with every retirement. Data debt is poor data quality, inconsistent definitions across systems, missing governance, siloed data that can't be integrated.

All four compound. Each shortcut makes the next decision harder, and teams become trapped in reactive mode — constantly paying interest but never paying principal.

In AEC, the project-based model makes debt accumulation structurally worse. Every project starts from scratch. Lessons don't transfer. Custom solutions proliferate. There is no incentive to invest in long-term foundations because no project fee covers it. The fix requires deliberate choices: reusable components and standards, project learnings captured in accessible formats, internal platforms that scale across projects, investment in tools even when clients won't pay directly for them.

Debt isn't always bad. Sometimes taking it on deliberately is the right strategic call: speed to market over perfect architecture, responding to a customer emergency over writing documentation. The problem is unintentional or untracked debt. Making debt visible — maintaining a backlog, estimating the interest each item costs, scheduling repayment — is what separates firms that compound capability from firms that compound problems.
notes.footerHtml:
Related: Every project makes the next one harder unless it doesn't, The data flywheel we haven't built yet, OpenClaw & institutional knowledge, Product management in complex organizations.

Sources:

  • https://aatir.substack.com/p/3-types-of-debt-product-teams-need

notes.title: Everything we build is a product
notes.file: Everything we build is a product.md
notes.excerpt: I keep coming back to something Brian Chesky said about industrial design: there are no product managers, because the designer *is* the product manager. You don't just decide how something looks, you own the question of
notes.bodyHtml:
I keep coming back to something Brian Chesky said about industrial design: there are no product managers, because the designer *is* the product manager. You don't just decide how something looks, you own the question of whether anyone actually needs it. A design that nobody buys isn't a near miss, it's a failure.

That stuck with me, because almost everything we make in construction is a product too. The building, of course, but also the model, the design workflow, the spec we hand to the next discipline. Each one is something we make for someone else to use. The difference is that our work is fragmented by discipline, so everyone owns their slice of the execution, and somehow nobody ends up owning the question of whether the thing should exist in that form at all.

I think that gap is about to matter a lot more, because of where AI is actually landing. Most firms are pointing it at the slice: automate the documentation, speed up the compliance check, halve the modelling time. All of that is real and useful, and I'm not dismissing it.

But it's worth noticing what Andrej Karpathy said recently when he called vibe coding already obsolete. What he described instead — writing the spec, supervising the plan, judging whether the output is actually right — is, as Jeff Gothelf pointed out, just product management wearing engineering vocabulary. Once execution stops being the bottleneck, the bottleneck quietly moves to deciding what is worth building in the first place.

That's the part I find genuinely interesting for our industry. We've spent a lot of energy speeding up the boring thing and treated that as the win. But the boring thing was never the hard part. The hard part was always the judgment we kept deferring, because we were too busy producing to stop and ask the better question. Automation doesn't hand that time back to us; it quietly promotes the whole industry into the product manager's job — deciding what's worth building — whether we asked for it or not.

Which reframes the risk. For a long time the danger in AEC was moving too slowly. Increasingly the danger is building the wrong thing faster and more confidently than before, and calling it progress because the dashboard says we were productive.

So the question I'd be sitting with, if I were deciding where AI goes in a firm, isn't how many tasks we can automate. It's a quieter one: as the work speeds up, who is actually responsible for deciding what's worth building at all — whether this model, this report, this workflow even needs to exist in the form we keep producing it. That role barely exists in AEC today, and it might be the one worth creating before we automate everything around it.
notes.footerHtml:
Related: The collapsing distance, More efficiency, more demand, Product management in complex organizations, Rigorous where it counts, When speed becomes a commodity.

Sources:

  • https://jeffgothelf.com/blog/karpathy-said-vibe-coding-is-obsolete-what-he-described-instead-is-product-management/
  • Brian Chesky, AI Founder Mode, Invest Like the Best EP.470

notes.title: Digitalization challenges
notes.excerpt: The biggest takeaway from the advancing computational design conference this year was that almost every AEC design firm is wrestling with what to call this job. Most firms understand the power and potential of computatio
notes.bodyHtml:
The biggest takeaway from the advancing computational design conference this year was that almost every AEC design firm is wrestling with what to call this job. Most firms understand the power and potential of computational design; very few have a concrete plan for how computational designers fit within their organisation.

The question isn't just a naming problem. It runs into how you attract, motivate, and create career paths that respect both the profession and the professional. Computational designers sit at an uncomfortable intersection — they're not quite engineers, not quite software developers, and not quite BIM managers. The firms that handle this best tend to define the role by its outcomes (what it produces for delivery teams) rather than by its methods (what tools it uses).

The broader challenge is that AEC firms are slow to digitalize not because the technology is insufficient, but because the organizational structures haven't caught up with what the technology makes possible.
notes.footerHtml:
Related: Computational design, Democratising computation, Skills aren't just for developers anymore, Appointing champions isn't a tech strategy.

notes.title: Corporate fear and startup agility
notes.excerpt: Corporations are often tempted to instruct their teams to "act more like a startup." This is usually a mistake. A corporation trying to act like a startup is like a marching band trying to play improvisational jazz — it
notes.bodyHtml:
Corporations are often tempted to instruct their teams to "act more like a startup." This is usually a mistake. A corporation trying to act like a startup is like a marching band trying to play improvisational jazz — it sounds exciting, but the result is chaos. Corporations and startups have genuinely different strengths: corporations offer coordination, efficiency, and resources; startups offer speed, nimbleness, and tolerance for failure.

Most large companies have accumulated substantial innovation debt. The activities that look like innovation — labs, sprints, hackathons — often produce no real learning or value. This is innovation theatre. The forest mismanagement analogy applies: everyone knows what needs to be done, but systemic incentives make it feel simultaneously easy and impossible to act on. The solution is controlled burns — deliberately tolerating bounded risk and inefficiency in the near term in exchange for long-term resilience.

Process accountability changes the conditions for learning. NASA's experience showed that an outcome-only culture breeds overconfidence in poor strategies. When each successful launch rewarded results, the faulty processes producing those results got repeated without scrutiny. Learning cultures score process as well as outcome: a positive result from a shallow process means you were lucky; a negative result from a deep, rigorous experiment means you generated valuable learning. Amazon's six-page memo externalises this — by writing out the problem, approaches considered, and how the solution serves the customer before a meeting starts, the quality of the process can be evaluated before results are known.

The structural reason corporations struggle with experimentation is that innovation is centralised and experimentation is expensive. Communication invites vetocracy: any single naysayer can kill a disruptive idea when consensus is required. Startups work differently — small empowered teams, cheap experiments, failure expected, incentives that reward upside rather than protect downside. The path for corporations isn't to become startups. It's to learn to collaborate with them, and to create internal conditions where controlled bets are possible.
notes.footerHtml: Related: Learning from Nvidia, Appointing champions isn't a tech strategy, Beyond RACI, Product discovery and feature factory.

notes.title: Configurators and infrastructure thinking
notes.excerpt: Configurators are rule-based design systems that generate solutions from predefined parameters, constraints, and component libraries. Their practical value is that they capture the tribal knowledge — the exact spacing, t
notes.bodyHtml:
Configurators are rule-based design systems that generate solutions from predefined parameters, constraints, and component libraries. Their practical value is that they capture the tribal knowledge — the exact spacing, tolerances, and unwritten rules — that is impossible to document in a single unified place. The hybrid approach that's emerging is to combine rule-based precision for the deterministic parts with AI's high-level pattern recognition for context and style. Construction has more modular elements than it behaves as if it does: plant rooms, bridges, tunnel systems, MEP assemblies. The industry's habit is to custom-design each one from scratch.

The business model argument matters here. Patric Hellermann at Foundamental points out that software is typically 2% of a construction company's P&L, with materials at 50% and labour and machinery at 48%. Optimising only the 2% while calling it digital transformation is optimising the wrong problem. The shift toward Outcome-as-a-Service — where construction companies purchase better, more cost-effective results rather than software licenses — reflects this. If your technology can deliver a project better and faster, demonstrate it by actually delivering. Don't promise transformation; back it up with results.

Infrastructure thinking is the design principle that connects these. Toby Li at Shopify described the choice as: build NFT gating in two weeks, or spend two months building a platform layer from which anyone could build NFT gating in one hour. Build the infrastructure that makes the feature trivial. Once that layer exists, many more scenarios emerge without each one requiring a fresh build. The mental model: write code against an API that doesn't exist yet — define the correct client code first, then implement the infrastructure that makes it possible.

Applied to AEC, this means asking for every build decision: is this an experiment, a feature, or infrastructure? Features take advantage of existing infrastructure. Infrastructure enables a whole class of features. An internal marketplace of micro tools — small, purpose-built utilities created by engineers and computational designers closest to the workflow problems — is the practical version. Each successful tool leaves reusable infrastructure behind. Successful internal solutions can then evolve into external products.
notes.footerHtml:
Related: Computational design, Platform, Democratising computation, The collapsing distance, AEC data and schemas.

Sources:

  • https://www.foundamental.com/perspectives/outcome-as-a-service
  • https://www.lennysnewsletter.com/p/how-shopify-builds-a-high-intensity-culture-farhan-thawar
  • https://e-brim.com/wp-content/uploads/BIM-for-Bridges-October-2024.pdf

notes.title: Computational design
notes.excerpt: Computational design should be treated as a capability system, not a set of isolated automation tasks. The goal is to move from reactive delivery support to a model that continuously improves how teams design, coordinate
notes.bodyHtml:
Computational design should be treated as a capability system, not a set of isolated automation tasks. The goal is to move from reactive delivery support to a model that continuously improves how teams design, coordinate, and make decisions.

The operating model that works is CDaaS — Computational Design as a Service — where a dedicated team provides computational design capability to delivery projects as a service, while systematically turning repeated work into reusable assets. It has four explicit responsibilities: service delivery (support projects with automation and decision support), workflow redesign (identify process bottlenecks and improve end-to-end flow), productisation (convert repeated solutions into stable templates and internal tools), and enablement (train teams, document standards, increase adoption). CDaaS solves immediate delivery problems and builds long-term capability at the same time.

The three-layer structure that makes this compound over time is: service layer first, then product layer, then knowledge layer. The service layer supports live projects and validates real-world needs quickly. The product layer converts proven service patterns into scalable internal tools and reusable components. The knowledge layer documents decisions and standards so each project improves the next one. The rule that holds the whole model together: every successful pilot should leave reusable infrastructure behind, not just a one-off fix.

Scaling follows a specific path: start with a small core team that delivers directly on projects. Once methods are stable, replicate through trained local teams using shared standards, playbooks, and tooling. The core team then shifts toward enablement, governance, and platform support. Asset-light scaling with centralized quality and learning.

The traps to avoid: building features without clear outcome hypotheses, treating computational teams as permanent firefighters, optimising only for efficiency while ignoring adoption, and adding disconnected tools instead of strengthening the ecosystem backbone.
notes.footerHtml:
Related: Configurators and infrastructure thinking, Democratising computation, Small Tools, Bigger Question, Every project makes the next one harder unless it doesn't.

Sources:

  • https://twitter.com/vwnd_/status/1497901961798111235

notes.title: Company as the product
notes.excerpt: In enterprise software, selling a product and selling a solution are not the same thing. A product is an artifact — it lets you automate some tasks, see analytics, comment on documents. A solution takes an existing proce
notes.bodyHtml:
In enterprise software, selling a product and selling a solution are not the same thing. A product is an artifact — it lets you automate some tasks, see analytics, comment on documents. A solution takes an existing process in a customer's organisation and transforms it into something that drives a meaningfully better outcome. The distinction matters because customers often struggle to close that gap themselves.

Qualtrics illustrates this clearly. The components — reporting, dashboards, analytics, surveys, sentiment analysis — are identical across different use cases. But a product manager trying to solve their specific problem with those components still needs help figuring out what to use and how. The Lego pieces alone aren't enough.

Apollo's root cause analysis program made this structural. They found that 80% of friction comes from 20% of issues, and those issues span the whole company: pricing and packaging, how credits work, onboarding, what the product promises vs what it delivers. None of those problems could be solved by product alone.

The insight that came out of that work is direct: you cannot solve those customer problems unless the company is thinking about itself as the product and is unified to go solve it. Marketing, sales, customer success, and product all need to be aligned on the same core users, use cases, and pain points.

With AI tools requiring significant process change on the customer side — as organisations shrink, roles shift, and outcomes become harder to define — this alignment becomes harder and more important at the same time. For the foreseeable future, the company is the product.
notes.footerHtml:
Related: Everything we build is a product, Product discovery and feature factory, Listening to customers, Why construction is trapped in delivery mode.

Sources:

  • https://podcasts.apple.com/au/podcast/product-growth-podcast/id1763555775?i=1000693612577

qualtrics illustrates this clearly. the components — reporting, dashboards, analytics, surveys, sentiment analysis — are identical across different use cases. but a product manager trying to solve their specific problem with those components still needs help figuring out what to use and how. the lego pieces alone aren't enough.
apollo's root cause analysis program made this structural. they found that 80% of friction comes from 20% of issues, and those issues span the whole company: pricing and packaging, how credits work, onboarding, what the product promises vs what it delivers. none of those problems could be solved by product alone.
the insight that came out of that work is direct: you cannot solve those customer problems unless the company is thinking about itself as the product and is unified to go solve it. marketing, sales, customer success, and product all need to be aligned on the same core users, use cases, and pain points.
with ai tools requiring significant process change on the customer side — as organisations shrink, roles shift, and outcomes become harder to define — this alignment becomes harder and more important at the same time. for the foreseeable future, the company is the product. related: everything we build is a product, product discovery and feature factory, listening to customers, why construction is trapped in delivery mode.

notes.title: Communication and clarity
notes.excerpt: AEC's biggest barrier to progress isn't technology — it's communication. The industry remains locked in a fear of sharing, dominated by command-and-control culture, even as a generational shift changes how technology is
notes.bodyHtml:
AEC's biggest barrier to progress isn't technology — it's communication. The industry remains locked in a fear of sharing, dominated by command-and-control culture, even as a generational shift changes how technology is perceived and expected. Reading *Paperless Builders* by Hamzah Shanbari reveals discouraging patterns: the same issues repeated at different scales, circling back through design, delivery, and construction. The core problem isn't what gets documented — it's that the why behind decisions, the reasoning for rethinking processes, the lessons from previous projects — stays locked in individuals' heads.

McKinsey's four fights framework for digital transformation maps the barriers directly. Fight ignorance: educate teams about what digital change actually means, using experiential techniques rather than generic training. Fight fear: address the anxieties that transformation triggers, build cultures where risk-taking and learning are safe. Fight guesswork: base decisions on data and pilots, not intuition. Fight diffusion: focus on big moves that create real value, not spreading resources thinly across disconnected initiatives.

The generational divide sharpens the communication challenge. Younger workers grew up with smartphones as an unremarkable baseline; they expect paperless workflows and streamlined processes. Veterans carry invaluable hands-on knowledge but learned through methods where extended administrative workdays were acceptable. Neither is wrong. Both are operating from their own rational baseline. Reverse mentorship programmes — where younger employees learn from seasoned professionals while also demonstrating new tools — close the gap in both directions without requiring either group to abandon what they know.

What the industry is still navigating is the early-majority crossing. Early adopters struggle to communicate correctly across the chasm because they're talking to people with a different relationship to risk, change, and proof. The message that works for the 15% who are already enthusiastic is almost never the message that works for the 50% who need to be convinced.
notes.footerHtml:
Related: Why technology adoption is poor in AEC, Appointing champions isn't a tech strategy, Building psychologically safe teams, The digital skills gap goes beyond tools.

Sources:

  • https://www.mckinsey.com/featured-insights/mckinsey-explainers/what-is-agile

notes.title: Building psychologically safe teams
notes.excerpt: There is no ideal organisational structure. Centralised models offer governance and less technical debt but move slowly and lack domain depth. Decentralised models give teams full control but create duplication, inconsis
notes.bodyHtml:
There is no ideal organisational structure. Centralised models offer governance and less technical debt but move slowly and lack domain depth. Decentralised models give teams full control but create duplication, inconsistency, and absence of standards. Federated and hub-and-spoke models can work well but require tightly integrated, aligned processes — a level of maturity most organisations don't have. The honest framing: each structure comes with a bag of sand you'll have to swallow every day. Starting centralised to build standards and best practices, then evolving toward hub-and-spoke as the model proves out, tends to work better than starting with the ideal end-state.

Ownership is one of the most powerful motivating forces available to a leader. It doesn't require large-scale reimagining of job descriptions. Allowing cross-functional teams flexibility and independence, letting people work on projects they can be proud of — these are low-cost moves with outsized effects on engagement and output quality.

The feature factory pattern is the structural opposite of psychological safety. Signs: success measured by features shipped, no time for discovery, decisions made by the highest-paid person's opinion, teams treated as delivery resources, innovation discouraged in favour of safe choices. The alternative isn't a management framework — it's an outcome: empower teams to focus on customer value and measure what actually changed.

Founder mode, as Paul Graham describes it, is the counterweight to over-delegation. Founders who stay deeply involved in operations — direct relationships throughout the organisation, willing to skip levels — get clarity rather than ambiguity. Brian Chesky: what everyone really wants is clarity. In exchange for some autonomy, they get a direction that's unambiguous and movement that's fast. The organisations that unlock collective genius are the ones where all important functions sit at the table — because the people without institutional knowledge are sometimes the most innovative, precisely because they don't know what's conventional.
notes.footerHtml:
Related: Beyond RACI, Learning from Nvidia, Corporate fear and startup agility, Appointing champions isn't a tech strategy.

Sources:

  • https://cutle.fish/blog/12-signs-youre-working-in-a-feature-factory
  • https://paulgraham.com/foundermode.html
  • https://medium.com/swlh/why-ownership-is-one-of-the-most-motivating-forces-there-is-651fc6c205ca

notes.title: Break docs down into smaller thought legos
notes.file: Break docs down into smaller thought legos.md
notes.excerpt: Most written documents are composed of a series of smaller idea chunks — usually paragraphs, each carrying one thought. The direct way to make knowledge reusable is to break those documents apart so that each idea chunk
notes.bodyHtml:
Most written documents are composed of a series of smaller idea chunks — usually paragraphs, each carrying one thought. The direct way to make knowledge reusable is to break those documents apart so that each idea chunk becomes an addressable block.

Writing is linear by nature. Authors force ideas into a sequence because that's how sentences and paragraphs work. But the ideas themselves aren't inherently linear. Once a document is decomposed into blocks, the same block can be referenced from multiple contexts without being copied, edited without breaking other references, and combined with other blocks in ways the original document didn't anticipate. The unit of reuse is the thought, not the document.

This is the principle behind block-reference mechanisms and, more broadly, behind tools like Obsidian's wiki-links: make the atomic unit of knowledge small enough to be recombined, not so large that it can only be read in one fixed order.
notes.footerHtml:
Related: Make readable to agents, The data flywheel we haven't built yet, OpenClaw & institutional knowledge.

Sources:

  • https://subconscious.substack.com/p/block-reference-mechanisms
