notes.title: Beyond RACI
notes.excerpt: When teams request a RACI chart, they're rarely asking for what they actually need. RACI is requested to solve collaboration problems, but it typically signals deeper dysfunction rather than resolving it. The framework a
notes.bodyHtml:
When teams request a RACI chart, they're rarely asking for what they actually need. RACI is requested to solve collaboration problems, but it typically signals deeper dysfunction rather than resolving it. The framework attempts to fix three underlying issues by documenting roles — and it misses all three.

The first is team structure: essential skills are missing, communication flows through hierarchies rather than directly, or leaders are working toward different visions. The second is mission clarity: when there's no shared understanding of purpose or strategic direction, uncertainty about what work needs doing creates confusion about who should do it — and assigning roles to the confusion doesn't resolve it. The third is handoff mentality: factory-era thinking that treats work as passing linearly from person to person, which conflicts with the iterative, collaborative nature of actual product development.

High-performing teams never request RACI. When collaboration works, team members naturally understand their contributions and adapt fluidly. A QA team member at Spotify prevented a poor product decision by pointing out that a proposed design merely pushed user confusion to the next screen. In a RACI environment, that input would never have emerged — QA isn't "responsible" for product design. It was possible because the team shared a mission and felt empowered to contribute wherever expertise could add value.

RACI should function as scaffolding, not infrastructure. Use it to surface confusion and facilitate conversations about roles, then explicitly discard it once understanding emerges. One approach: create the matrix before an offsite, discuss what it revealed, then physically throw it away and establish a more flexible working agreement. The moment RACI becomes a permanent document, it creates rigidity that inhibits exactly the adaptive collaboration it was meant to enable.

The terminology doesn't help. Responsible and Accountable sound synonymous in everyday language; RACI assigns them different meanings that require memorisation. Consulted sounds inclusive; in practice it delays decisions while adding the illusion of participation. Frameworks that focus on decision-making rather than general responsibilities tend to work better.
notes.footerHtml:
Related: Building psychologically safe teams, Appointing champions isn't a tech strategy, Communication and clarity, Product management in complex organizations.

Sources:

  • https://thesearesystems.substack.com/p/task-estimation-conquering-hofstadters

notes.title: Appointing champions isn't a tech strategy
notes.file: Appointing champions isn't a tech strategy.md
notes.excerpt: Construction has had an answer to technology adoption for years: put a champion in the room. Someone who leans forward in demos, installs tools on their own time, comes back with use cases. Give them a title — AI champio
notes.bodyHtml:
Construction has had an answer to technology adoption for years: put a champion in the room. Someone who leans forward in demos, installs tools on their own time, comes back with use cases. Give them a title — AI champion, digital lead — and the adoption problem feels handled.

Maryrose Lyons told the story on Bricks and Bytes. One company had twelve of them. They met every month, talked about new tools, spun up prototypes on Lovable, and clearly enjoyed it. But after a couple of years the business still worked exactly the way it had before — which is why they brought her in. That's not an unusual story, it's the pattern.

Those people are real, and that's the point. They're the ones trialling and learning and pushing the boundaries of how to use emerging tech to solve old problems. They believe in this, and that belief is valuable.

But identifying a champion is not a strategy — it's the beginning of one.

The firms that actually move give champions something harder: a small focused team instead of one person per department, two or three measurable use cases with clear accountability, and regular refreshes as the tools evolve.

Maryrose went back to a company she'd worked with years earlier and found them doing things exactly as she'd left them. The technology had moved on; they hadn't. The champion had simply maintained.

That's not a people problem, it's a structural one. A champion without direction, clear outcomes, or leadership that actively creates space for experimentation will eventually stop pushing — or become the person who runs the monthly meeting about tools they're not allowed to deploy.

The goal was never to find people willing to try — plenty of firms have those. The harder part is building the conditions that let them change something. Seen that way, the champion question starts to look less like who you appoint and more like what you're willing to restructure around them.
notes.footerHtml: Related: Corporate fear and startup agility, Building psychologically safe teams, Why technology adoption is poor in AEC, Rigorous where it counts.

notes.title: AEC data and schemas
notes.excerpt: The core direction for AEC data strategy is to move away from monolithic, file-centric workflows toward structured, interoperable, object-level data models that support cross-tool collaboration and automation. Files are
notes.bodyHtml:
The core direction for AEC data strategy is to move away from monolithic, file-centric workflows toward structured, interoperable, object-level data models that support cross-tool collaboration and automation. Files are not the right unit of exchange. Objects are.

When workflows target data structures rather than authoring tool boundaries, sharing, composition, and downstream automation become possible. Entity identification and change tracking — knowing what changed, at what level, and by whom — become the central technical problems in multi-party delivery. These are solvable, but only if schema design is treated as a first-order concern rather than an afterthought.

Schema quality is foundational: robust structures make AI, reliable analytics, and interoperability possible; poor structure makes all three unreliable. The AI readiness of AEC data is not primarily an AI problem. It is a schema problem. Models that get trained or queried against inconsistent, poorly structured data produce inconsistent, unreliable outputs. Getting the schema right first is not bureaucracy — it's the prerequisite.

Open standards reduce lock-in and conversion overhead. IFC has been the industry's attempt at a universal adapter, and while it remains incomplete, the instinct is correct. The alternative — proprietary representations that require lossy conversion at every handoff — is what has kept AEC data fragmented for decades. Data governance and legal-operational alignment should support interoperability, not block it; the instinct to protect proprietary formats is understandable but counterproductive at the ecosystem level.
notes.footerHtml:
Related: Make readable to agents, The data flywheel we haven't built yet, Context problem, PDFs as the source of knowledge, Configurators and infrastructure thinking.
